title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.

content.title: The Drive to Win: Decoding Athletic Motivation
content.subtitle: Why the source of an athlete's focus determines their long-term success.
content.text: Motivation is the fuel of athletic endeavor, yet not all fuel burns with the same efficiency. In sport psychology, researchers distinguish between intrinsic motivation—doing something for the inherent satisfaction of the activity—and extrinsic motivation—acting to achieve an external reward or avoid punishment. [31] While the lure of fame and financial gain can provide a powerful initial spark, studies suggest that athletes driven primarily by external factors are more prone to burnout and performance anxiety. [/31] The most resilient competitors are usually those who find joy in the process of mastery itself, rather than just the result on the scoreboard. || This leads to the 'Self-Determination Theory,' which posits that for an athlete to stay motivated, they need to feel three things: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Autonomy is the feeling of being in control of one’s training; competence is the sense of getting better at the craft; and relatedness is the connection to a team or coach. [32] When a coach becomes too controlling, they inadvertently undermine the athlete's sense of autonomy, which can lead to a significant drop in spontaneous effort and creative problem-solving during a match. [/32] A supportive environment is one that empowers the athlete to take ownership of their own development. || Another critical concept is 'Achievement Goal Theory.' Athletes generally fall into two categories: those with a 'task orientation' and those with an 'ego orientation.' [33] Task-oriented individuals define success as personal improvement and learning, whereas ego-oriented individuals judge their success solely by how they compare to others. [/33] The danger of a pure ego orientation is that it makes confidence fragile. If an athlete's self-worth is tied to being 'the best,' a single loss to a superior opponent can shatter their motivation. Conversely, a task-oriented athlete sees a loss as data—an opportunity to identify weaknesses and refine their technique. || [34] The author’s attitude toward the 'no pain, no gain' culture in modern sports is one of critical concern. [/34] While hard work is essential, this mantra often ignores the psychological necessity of rest and recovery. Constant high-intensity training without mental breaks can lead to 'overtraining syndrome,' characterized by chronic fatigue and a loss of competitive drive. True excellence requires a sophisticated understanding of the body’s limits. It is a marathon of consistency, not a series of desperate sprints that end in injury or mental exhaustion. || [35] Some sports commentators argue that 'clutch' players—those who perform best under extreme pressure—are born with a special gene for calmness. [/35] However, sport psychologists argue that 'clutch' performance is actually the result of 'stress inoculation training.' By gradually exposing athletes to increasing levels of pressure in practice, they learn to interpret physiological arousal (a racing heart, sweaty palms) as excitement rather than fear. They don't have fewer nerves; they simply have a better relationship with them. || Ultimately, the secret to longevity in sports is a balanced motivational profile. [36] Athletes who can align their personal values with their professional goals are the ones who manage to stay at the top of their game for decades. [/36] They recognize that the trophy is just a temporary object, but the character built through the pursuit of excellence is a permanent gain. The most successful athletes are those who play because they love the game, even when the lights of the stadium have gone out.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that extrinsic motivation...
options: A) can lead to a higher risk of psychological exhaustion.
B) is more efficient than intrinsic motivation in the long run.
C) has no impact on an athlete's initial interest in a sport.
D) is the primary reason why elite athletes find joy in their sport.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31], stating that those driven by external factors are 'more prone to burnout and performance anxiety.'

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, what happens when a coach is overly controlling?
options: A) The athlete's natural drive and effort may decrease.
B) The athlete feels a stronger connection to the team.
C) The athlete's sense of competence increases significantly.
D) The athlete becomes more creative in their problem-solving.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32], stating that undermining autonomy leads to a 'drop in spontaneous effort.'

questions.question: What is the main difference between 'task-oriented' and 'ego-oriented' athletes?
options: A) Task-oriented athletes only care about the final scoreboard.
B) Ego-oriented athletes have more stable long-term confidence.
C) Ego-oriented athletes view losses as opportunities to learn.
D) Task-oriented athletes focus on self-improvement rather than comparison.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33], stating task-oriented individuals define success as 'personal improvement and learning.'

questions.question: How does the author feel about the 'no pain, no gain' philosophy?
options: A) He believes it is the only way to achieve true excellence.
B) He is worried that it overlooks the importance of psychological rest.
C) He is indifferent to its impact on an athlete's physical limits.
D) He thinks it is a helpful mantra for avoiding overtraining syndrome.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34], expressing 'critical concern' and noting the mantra 'ignores the psychological necessity of rest.'

questions.question: The author mentions 'stress inoculation training' to argue that...
options: A) 'clutch' players are simply lucky during high-pressure moments.
B) athletes should avoid any form of physiological arousal.
C) elite performance is an inherited genetic trait.
D) the ability to handle pressure is a skill that can be practiced.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35], arguing that clutch performance is the result of training that helps athletes interpret arousal as excitement.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, what does the author conclude about successful athletes?
options: A) Their longevity is based on a deep, personal connection to their sport.
B) They only play as long as they are receiving external rewards.
C) They stay at the top by separating their personal values from their goals.
D) They prioritize temporary trophies over personal character.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36], stating that aligning personal values with goals allows athletes to 'stay at the top of their game for decades.'
